title: Cilium over Calico
context: Needed a CNI for the homelab K8s cluster that supports advanced network policies, observability, and eBPF-based dataplane.
decision: Chose Cilium for its eBPF-based networking, built-in Hubble observability, and CiliumNetworkPolicy CRDs which are more expressive than vanilla NetworkPolicy.
consequences: Better L7 visibility, eBPF performance gains, native support for identity-based policies. Trade-off: higher memory footprint than Calico, steeper learning curve.

title: Vault HA over Sealed Secrets
context: Needed secrets management for K8s workloads. Options: Sealed Secrets (simple, GitOps-native), Vault (full-featured, HA, dynamic secrets).
decision: Chose HashiCorp Vault in HA mode (3-replica Raft) with External Secrets Operator to sync secrets into K8s. Sealed Secrets was simpler but lacked rotation, audit logging, and dynamic credentials.
consequences: Full audit trail, automatic secret rotation, dynamic database credentials. Trade-off: operational overhead of running Vault HA, more complex disaster recovery.

title: ArgoCD over Flux
context: Needed a GitOps operator to manage 22+ applications across multiple namespaces from a Git repo.
decision: Chose ArgoCD for its UI, Application/ApplicationSet CRDs, SSO integration, and RBAC model. Flux is lighter but the ArgoCD dashboard gives immediate visibility into sync state across all apps.
consequences: Single pane of glass for all deployments. App-of-Apps pattern scales well. Trade-off: heavier resource footprint than Flux, UI can become a crutch vs. CLI-first workflows.

title: Longhorn over OpenEBS
context: Needed distributed block storage for stateful workloads (PostgreSQL, Redis) on bare-metal K8s with data replication.
decision: Chose Longhorn for its simplicity, built-in UI, snapshot/backup to S3 (MinIO), and 2-replica configuration. OpenEBS cStor was more complex to operate.
consequences: Easy backup to TrueNAS MinIO, volume snapshots for DR, automatic replica rebuilds. Trade-off: lower IOPS than local-path or OpenEBS Mayastor for high-throughput workloads.

title: Go for Linkvolt Microservices
context: Building a link-in-bio SaaS with 6 microservices. Needed a language that produces small container images, handles concurrency well, and compiles to a single binary.
decision: Chose Go for its small binary size (scratch/distroless images ~15MB), goroutine concurrency model, fast compile times, and excellent K8s ecosystem (client-go, controller-runtime).
consequences: Tiny container images, fast cold starts, easy to deploy as static binaries. Trade-off: more verbose than Python for quick prototyping, less library ecosystem for web UI.
